inputs.label: Direct Plan Return (%)
inputs.hint: Expected annual return of the direct plan

inputs.label: Expense Ratio Difference (%)
inputs.hint: Regular plan has this much higher expense ratio (typically 0.5–2%)

staticTable.caption: Direct vs Regular plan — extra wealth from direct plan at 12% return with 1.5% expense ratio gap

examples.heading: ₹10 lakh invested for 20 years — direct vs regular plan at 12%
examples.text: Investing ₹10 lakhs in a direct plan at 12% annual return for 20 years grows to approximately ₹96.5 lakhs. The same investment in a regular plan with a 1.5% higher expense ratio (effectively 10.5% return) grows to only ₹74.1 lakhs. The difference is ₹22.4 lakhs — money that went to the distributor as commission rather than to the investor. Over 20 years, the expense ratio gap compounds into a massive wealth difference.

examples.heading: The 1% cost of advice — when regular plans might be worth it
examples.text: A financial advisor charging 1% on a regular plan provides value if their guidance helps you: avoid panic-selling in downturns; maintain appropriate asset allocation; stay invested for the full duration; and select better-performing funds. If an advisor helps you achieve 13% instead of 12% through better fund selection, the regular plan with advisor may outperform a self-managed direct plan.

decisionGuide.question: Should I invest in direct or regular mutual fund plans?
decisionGuide.answer: Choose direct plans if you are comfortable researching and selecting funds yourself, can maintain investment discipline without an advisor, and are investing ₹5 lakhs or more where the expense ratio savings are substantial. Choose regular plans if you need a qualified financial advisor (CFP) to manage your portfolio and can verify their value adds more than their cost.

decisionGuide.question: Is it worth switching from regular to direct plan?
decisionGuide.answer: Switching from regular to direct plan is treated as a redemption and fresh purchase for tax purposes. If your holding is long-term (LTCG), the tax cost of switching may be low. For a ₹10 lakh investment with ₹5 lakhs in gains, switching would trigger approximately ₹46,875 in LTCG tax. Compare this against the estimated annual savings from direct plan to determine the break-even period.

faq.q: What is the difference between direct and regular mutual fund plans?
faq.a: Direct plans are bought directly from the fund house without any intermediary, resulting in a lower expense ratio (typically 0.5–1.5% less than regular plans). Regular plans involve a distributor or financial advisor who receives commission from the fund house, funded by the higher expense ratio. Both plans invest in the same portfolio — the only difference is the expense ratio, which directly impacts your returns.

faq.q: How much extra do I earn from direct plans over 10 years?
faq.a: On a ₹10 lakh investment at 12% direct plan return with a 1.5% expense gap (10.5% regular plan return) over 10 years: direct plan = ₹31.06 lakhs; regular plan = ₹26.6 lakhs. Difference = ₹4.46 lakhs. On a ₹25 lakh investment over 20 years at the same rates: direct plan = ₹2.41 crore; regular plan = ₹1.86 crore. Difference = ₹55 lakhs. The compounding of expense ratio savings is enormous over long periods.

faq.q: What is the typical expense ratio for direct vs regular plans?
faq.a: For equity mutual funds: direct plans typically have an expense ratio of 0.5–1.5%; regular plans have 1.5–2.5%. The gap is typically 0.5–1.5 percentage points. For large-cap index funds, direct plans can have an expense ratio as low as 0.05–0.1% (Nifty 50 index funds), making the gap with regular plans even wider. For debt funds, the gap is smaller — typically 0.3–0.5%.

faq.q: Can I switch from regular to direct plan online?
faq.a: Yes. You can switch from regular to direct plan online through: (1) The fund house's website or app; (2) MF Central or MF Utility portals; (3) Your broker's app if they support direct plans. The switch is a redemption from regular plan and fresh investment in direct plan — it may trigger capital gains tax. Consider timing the switch to minimise tax impact, ideally after the holding period qualifies for LTCG treatment.

faq.q: Are direct plans always better than regular plans?
faq.a: Direct plans are mathematically superior in terms of expense ratio. However, if a financial advisor helps you make better investment decisions — avoiding poor funds, maintaining discipline during volatility, rebalancing at the right time — the value they add may exceed their 0.5–1.5% annual cost. The question is whether your advisor consistently adds more value than their fees. For index fund investors with a buy-and-hold strategy, direct plans are almost always better.

faq.q: Do direct plans have a higher NAV than regular plans?
faq.a: Yes. Since direct plans have a lower expense ratio, their NAV grows faster than regular plans for the same fund. Over time, the NAV of a direct plan will always be higher than the corresponding regular plan. This higher NAV is not a disadvantage — you simply buy fewer units at a higher price, but the underlying value per unit is genuinely higher. Returns calculated in percentage terms are what matter, not absolute NAV.

howToSteps.name: Enter Your Investment Amount
howToSteps.text: Enter the lumpsum amount you are comparing. The calculator shows the 20-year difference, so even small investment differences in expense ratio become significant.

howToSteps.name: Set Direct Plan Return
howToSteps.text: Enter the expected return of the direct plan fund you are considering. Use the fund's historical 5-year or 10-year CAGR as a reference, keeping in mind past returns do not guarantee future performance.

howToSteps.name: Set Expense Ratio Gap
howToSteps.text: Enter the difference in expense ratio between the regular and direct plan. Check the fund's factsheet — the gap is typically 0.5–1.5% for equity funds. For Nifty 50 index funds, the gap may be smaller.

howToSteps.name: Compare Long-Term Difference
howToSteps.text: The calculator shows the corpus for both plans and the extra wealth you accumulate by choosing the direct plan. This difference is the commission that would otherwise go to distributors.
